a: The mean is the everyday average: add up all the numbers, then divide by how many numbers there are. It gives you one number that represents the 'middle' or typical size of the whole group.
fs: The mean is the sum of values divided by the count; it balances the data but gets dragged toward extreme values.
fj: Assuming the mean is always the 'typical' value. A few huge outliers can pull it far from where most data sits.

a: The median is the middle value when you line all the numbers up from smallest to largest. Half the data sits below it and half sits above it. If there are two middle numbers, you average those two.
fs: The median is the 50th percentile; it ignores how extreme the outliers are, so it resists being skewed by them.
fj: Confusing median with mean. On skewed data (like salaries) they can be very different numbers.

a: Standard deviation tells you how spread out your numbers are around the mean. A small value means most points sit close to the average; a large value means they are scattered far apart.
fs: Standard deviation is the typical distance of a value from the mean; it's the square root of the variance.
fj: Thinking a higher mean means more spread. Spread and average are separate: two groups can share a mean yet differ wildly in spread.

a: Probability is a number from 0 to 1 that says how likely something is to happen. 0 means it never happens, 1 means it always happens, and 0.5 means a 50-50 chance, like a coin flip.
fs: Probability measures likelihood on a 0-to-1 scale; the chances of all possible outcomes always add up to 1.
fj: Believing past results change the next independent event. A fair coin landing heads 5 times still has a 0.5 chance next flip.

a: A distribution describes how often each value (or range of values) shows up in your data. Picture a chart where tall bars mean 'this value happens a lot' and short bars mean 'this value is rare'.
fs: A distribution is the shape of the data: which values are common, which are rare, and how spread out they are.
fj: Assuming all data follows a bell curve. Many real-world distributions are skewed, lopsided, or have multiple peaks.
